elements.sent-2411-001: The goal of this document is to describe how we will simulate CDC funds for the purposes of our briefing notes.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our aim is to provide a description of a single-employer scheme that closely mirrors the Royal Mail CDC scheme and a multi-employer scheme that reflects industry understanding of how such a scheme is likely to operate.
elements.sent-2411-003: In addition, wherever we are simplifying matters for ease of modelling, this document should explicitly note that.
elements.sent-2411-004: This document seeks to define the {\em contract of these schemes.}
elements.sent-2411-005: The fund will make investments whose returns are unpredictable, similarly the mortality of investors is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-007: Some of the parameters used to define the contract are determined by economic and actuarial forecasts.
elements.sent-2411-008: For example the parameters of an annuity contract describe how much you receive each year and how much the annuity costs and depend upon projected growth of gilts and projected mortality.
elements.sent-2411-009: A completely unambiguous specification of the contract should include details of how such parameters will be computed as this will affect the benefits received.
elements.sent-2411-010: We will describe how the parameters can be computed from actuarial projections, but won't consider how the actuarial projections themselves are selected.
elements.sent-2411-011: One simplification we shall make throughout is to assume that all payments into and out of the scheme happen once a year.
elements.sent-2411-012: We will assume that all benefit-cuts and bonuses are made immediately, although the legislation allows some phasing-in of benefit cuts.
elements.section-2411-003: General equations for all CDC funds
elements.sent-2411-013: We will assume there are $M$ distinct types of individual in a CDC fund- possibly different ages, or different demographic factor.
elements.sent-2411-014: At any time, $t$, ($t \in {\mathbb Z _{\geq 0}$) each type of individual $\xi$ ($0\leq \xi \leq M-1$) has accrued a nominal benefit amount $B^{\xi,\cum _t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Individuals of type $\xi$ who have retired will receive this as their pension in year $t$, but for individuals who have not yet retired it represents their currently accrued entitlement.
elements.sent-2411-016: We write ${\mathbf 1 ^{R,\xi _t$ to be the indicator function taking the value $1$ if individuals of type $\xi$ have retired at time $t$ and $0$ otherwise.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: So the pension received by individuls of type $\xi$ is ${\mathbf 1 ^{R,\xi _t B^{\xi,\cum _t$ The initial entitlements satisfy \begin{equation}B^{\xi,\cum}_0=0\end{equation} for all $\xi$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Each year, a growth rate $h_t$ above CPI growth ($i_t$) is selected, together with a bonus/benefit-cut level of $\theta_t$.
elements.sent-2411-019: The intention is that in normal years $\theta_t=1$, indicating no bonus or benefit cut, but if assets and liabilities become too mismatched, benefit cuts or bonuses may be awarded.
elements.sent-2411-020: An individual's benefit entitlement before new contributions are added is given by \begin{equation}B^{\xi,\cum}_{t-}:= (1+ i_t + h_t) \theta_t B^{\xi,\cum}_{t-1} \quad \text{if }t>0. \label{eq:cdcDefining}\end{equation} This growth rate is selected to ensure that a central estimate of the future liabilities matches the asset value of the fund before new contributions are added, $A_{t-}$, as we will now explain.
elements.sent-2411-021: We write $p^\xi(t_1,\ell)$ for the probability of an individual of type $\xi$ surviving to time $t_1+\ell$ given that they are alive at time $t_1$ Each type of individual $\xi$, has an associated life-styling strategy, and this gives rise to an associated discount rate $P^\xi(t_1,\ell)$ for payments received at $t_1+\ell$ which we will use when calculating liabilities to these individuals.
elements.sent-2411-022: The investment strategy of the fund as a whole will be a liability-weighted combination of the investment strategies for each individual.
elements.sent-2411-023: At each time point, $t$ we have a projection $i(t,\ell)$ for CPI at the future time-point $t+\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-024: We write $N^\xi_t$ for the number of surviving individuals in the fund of type $\xi$ at time $t$, all of whom receive a pension that year.
elements.sent-2411-025: We require for $t>0$ that \begin{equation}A_{t-} = \theta_t \sum_{\xi=0}^{M-1} \sum_{\ell=0}^\infty I_t(h,\ell) P^\xi(t,\ell) N^\xi_t B^{\xi,\cum}_{t-1} p^\xi(t,\ell) {\mathbf 1}^{R,\xi}_{t+\ell}. \label{eq:cdcGrowth}\end{equation} The discount rate satisfies \begin{equation}P^\xi(t,\ell) = \prod_{s=t, s<\ell} (1+r_{P,\xi,t, s})\end{equation} and the incrememt I is given by \begin{equation}I_t(h,\ell) = \prod_{s=t, s<\ell} (1 + i(t,\ell) + h)\end{equation} where $r_{P,\xi, t_1, s}$ is the rate of return predicted at time $t_1$ on assets over the period $(s, s+1)$ when the assets are invested with lifestyling appropriate for individuals of type $\xi$.
elements.sent-2411-026: This lifestyling is performed on the basis of age and we will write $\pi_a$ for the proportion allocated to growth assets when investing to match the liabilities of investors of age $a$.
elements.sent-2411-027: The equation \eqref{eq:cdcGrowth determines $h_t$ and $\theta_t$ for $t> 0$ in a way that we will now describe.}
elements.sent-2411-028: First we try to solve the equation with $\theta_t=1$.
elements.sent-2411-029: If the resulting value is greater than some upper bound $h^+$, we cap $h$ and award a one-off benefit payment.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this case $h_t$=$h^+$ and $\theta_t$ is chosen to ensure that \eqref{eq:cdcGrowth holds.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Similarly, if we find that the nominal growth $h^n_t:=i(t,0)+h_t$ is negative, we choose $h_t=-i(t,0)$ and apply a benefit cut by choosing $\theta_t$ so that equation \eqref{eq:cdcGrowth holds.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Each type of individual makes a contribution $C^\xi_t$ at time $t$ and receives an additional benefit amount $B^\xi_t$.. So \begin{equation}B^{\xi,\cum}_t = B^{\xi,\cum}_{t-}+B^\xi_t.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: In a single-employer scheme, the contribution levels and associated benefit are fixed by the employer, see the next section for a specific model.
elements.sent-2411-034: In a multi-employer scheme the benefit amount is computed from the contribution using the equation: \begin{equation}\label{eq:multiemployerBenefit} C^\xi_t = \sum_{\ell=0}^\infty I_t(h,\ell) P^\xi(t,\ell) B^{\xi}_t p^\xi(t,\ell) {\mathbf 1}^{R,\xi}_{t+\ell }.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: The assets of the fund after new payments have been received and annual pension payments have been made is denoted $A_{t+}$ and is computed as \begin{equation}A_{t+} := A_{t-} + \sum_{\xi=0}^{M-1} N^\xi_t (C^\xi_t - B^{\xi,\cum}_t {\mathbf 1}^{R,\xi}_t) \label{eq:increments}\end{equation} In a multi-employer fund, we will automatically have that assets and liabilities (computed using the central estimate) also match after payments have been received and made: \begin{equation}A_{t+} = \sum_{\xi=0}^{M-1} \sum_{\ell=1}^\infty (1+i(t,l)+h_t)^{{\ell}} P(t,\ell) N^\xi_t B^{\xi,\cum}_{t} p^\xi(t,\ell) {\mathbf 1}^{R,\xi}_{t+\ell}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-040: We will use a model where the type of an individual $\xi$ is determined by their age.
elements.sent-2411-041: The earliest one can join the fund is at age $x$, an individual receives their first pension payment at age $x+T$ and is certain to be dead at age $x+\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-042: At the start of our simulation, we will assume that there is an equal number of individuals contributing to the fund of all ages from $x$ up to $X$.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the preprint \cite{catherineDonnelly , it is assumed $x=X$, but we will use the more realistic choice $X=T+x-1$ when presenting our results.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Each year, a new generation of individuals age $x$ arrive of the same size.
elements.sent-2411-045: Hence we can write down the details of the various indicator functions using this notation. \begin{equation}\label{eq:ageAndXi} \begin{split} \text{age}(\xi,t) :=X-\xi+t \mathbf{1}^{R,\xi}_t := \mathbf{1}_{\text{age}(\xi,t) \geq T+ x} \mathbf{1}^{C,\xi}_t := \mathbf{1}_{\text{age}(\xi,t) \in [x,T+x)} \end{split}\end{equation} The equations above have $\beta_t$ and $\alpha_t$ depending upon time, but the aim is for them to remain as constant as long-term forecasts of factors such as mortality allow.

elements.sent-2411-078: We evaluate the performance of and level of intergenerational cross subsidy in single-employer and multi-employer collective defined contribution (CDC) schemes which have been designed to be compatible with UK legislation.
elements.sent-2411-079: The single-employer scheme captures the essential features of the Royal Mail CDC scheme, which is currently the only UK CDC scheme.
elements.sent-2411-080: We find that the schemes can be successful in smoothing pension outcomes while outperforming a DC + annuity scheme, but that this outperformance is not guaranteed in a single-employer scheme.
elements.sent-2411-081: There are significant intergenerational cross-subsidies in the single-employer scheme.
elements.sent-2411-082: These qualitatively mirror the cross-subsidies seen in existing defined benefit schemes, but we find the magnitude of the cross-subsidies is much larger in single-employer CDC schemes.
elements.sent-2411-083: The multi-employer scheme is intended to minimize such cross-subsidies, but we find that such subsidies still arise due to the approximate pricing methodology implicit in the scheme design.
elements.sent-2411-084: These cross-subsidies tend to cancel out over time, but in any given year they can be large, implying that it is important to use a rigorous pricing methodology when valuing collective pension investments.
elements.sent-2411-085: Collective Defined Contribution (CDC) pension schemes are arriving in the UK, after a long, uncertain beginning \cite{wilkinson .}
elements.sent-2411-086: These are pension schemes in which the contribution rate is fixed, as a percentage of salaries, and the benefits paid out vary.
elements.sent-2411-087: The idea is that the scheme members share both investment and longevity risk in order to have a better outcome in retirement.
elements.sent-2411-088: The adjustment lever in a CDC pension scheme, that benefits are adjusted to keep the contributions unchanged, is different to that in defined benefit (DB) pension schemes, in which contributions are adjusted to keep the benefits unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-089: The CDC scheme uses the same lever as a defined contribution (DC) pension scheme.
elements.sent-2411-090: In this paper we study two different CDC scheme designs.
elements.sent-2411-091: The first design we consider is a single-employer scheme closely modelled on that of the Royal Mail CDC plan.
elements.sent-2411-092: This is the first CDC scheme to launch in the UK and has been the driving force behind the introduction of the legislation enabling CDC schemes in the UK \cite{wilkinson .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Combined with the scale of the scheme, which is anticipated to have well over 100,000 members, the importance and significance of the Royal Mail CDC is clear.
elements.sent-2411-094: It is the prototype presented by the actuaries involved in its design and testing to other actuaries.
elements.sent-2411-095: This scheme is designed as a replacement for a previous DB scheme and so is intended to produce a similar profile of contributions and benefits to that scheme.
elements.sent-2411-096: The second design we will consider is a multi-employer CDC scheme.
elements.sent-2411-097: No such scheme exists in the UK at present, so we develop a scheme design which we believe reflects the common understanding of UK pension professionals on how multi-employer CDC might operate.
elements.sent-2411-098: To achieve this, we have worked with the Pension Policy Institute who have conducted a series of interviews on CDC pensions with a broad range of stakeholders and who have used these insights to publish two briefing notes on CDC.
elements.sent-2411-099: In addition, we have used the expertise of a project advisory group drawn from across the pension industry and academia.
elements.sent-2411-100: The consensus is that multi-employer scheme design is intended to minimize cross-subsidies between generations to prevent one employer potentially subsidizing others.
elements.sent-2411-101: It is also envisioned as a DC replacement rather than as a DB replacement, which too implies there should be low levels of intergenerational cross subsidies.
elements.sent-2411-102: Owadally et al. \cite{owadally discuss comprehensively CDC plans in the UK with respect to policy, as well as their risks and the ensuing advantages and disadvantages.}
elements.sent-2411-103: They model an earlier proposal for the Royal Mail CDC plan, but the scheme design has since changed.
elements.sent-2411-104: They also compare the outcomes for a single member of their model mature CDC plan against typical DC-based alternative pension schemes.
elements.sent-2411-105: They find their CDC plan can pay higher and more stable pensions compared to the alternative schemes considered.
elements.sent-2411-106: This work is an attempt to understand better the broad implications of the Royal Mail CDC plan, and to compare them to multi-employer CDC.
elements.sent-2411-107: We capture the broad design of the Royal Mail scheme, but not all of it.
elements.sent-2411-108: In particular, death and spousal benefits are omitted and we assume that benefit cuts and bonuses that must occur when the fund is significantly over- or under-funded take affect immediately.
elements.sent-2411-109: Our mortality modelling is also simplified: we assume no systematic longevity risk and that the fund is large enough for individual longevity risk to be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-110: We do not consider any heterogeneity in the multi-employer fund in our simulations in this paper, but we do show how that can be incorporated into the scheme design if desired.
elements.sent-2411-111: We have discussed results from simulating heterogeneous funds in \cite{upton .}
elements.sent-2411-112: The collective risk-sharing in both CDC schemes we consider is done through annual pension increases.
elements.sent-2411-113: Every year, a pension increase on accrued benefits is declared, and the increased pension amount becomes the accrued benefit of each member.
elements.sent-2411-114: The same annual pension increase is declared on all members’ accrued pension.
elements.sent-2411-115: It is this uniform declaration which makes the scheme a collective risk-sharing one.
elements.sent-2411-116: We will refer to our schemes as {\em shared-indexation schemes to highlight this common feature of their design.}
elements.sent-2411-117: It is important to note that these schemes are not the only possible CDC designs.
elements.sent-2411-118: For example, post-retirement-only CDC schemes which operate as pooled annuity funds have gained increasing attention from the UK industry.
elements.sent-2411-119: Even within whole-life schemes, there are many different possible designs.
elements.sent-2411-120: It is also true that the ‘best’ plan from a purely financial point of view, is probably not the ‘best’ plan if no-one wants it.
elements.sent-2411-121: It may also not be the best plan if it is so complicated that members do not understand what they will receive.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, this paper does not consider these aspects and instead focuses on the financial characteristics of shared-indexation schemes.
elements.sent-2411-123: In particular, the focus is on intergenerational risk-sharing.
elements.sent-2411-124: Intergenerational risk-sharing is a fundamental part of CDC schemes.
elements.sent-2411-125: It is also the most controversial.
elements.sent-2411-126: The idea is that risks, such as volatile investment returns and uncertain future lifetimes, are shared across the generations of the pension scheme.
elements.sent-2411-127: If this can be done fairly, then all generations with the same risk characteristics will face the same risk-return trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-128: We will see that a dominant source of cross-subsidy in the single-employer scheme results from the fixed contribution rate and nominal-benefit which is accrued each year.
elements.sent-2411-129: This favours investors who are close to retirement as they receive the same benefit as younger investors for the same contribution, despite their being less time for their investments to grow.
elements.sent-2411-130: This mirrors the behaviour of the DB scheme that the single-employer scheme is designed to replace.
elements.sent-2411-131: Thus, these cross-subsidies should be seen as an intentional design feature of the single-employer scheme.
elements.sent-2411-132: However, we find that the level of cross-subsidy is considerably higher in a single-employer scheme than a DB scheme.
elements.sent-2411-133: An age-related benefit accrual would reduce the cost of this anomaly on later generations.
elements.sent-2411-134: The lack of financial fairness on an accrued basis distinguishes a Royal Mail-type CDC scheme from those studied in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-135: For example, \cite{haan , which shows a greater financial fairness of their studied collective scheme, is based on CDC plans which are seen in the Netherlands.}
elements.sent-2411-136: The multi-employer scheme is designed to avoid such obvious subsidies and is instead intended to be actuarially fair in the sense that each year members' contributions match their new benefit entitlements.
elements.sent-2411-137: However, the implicit pricing methodology used to achieve this is only approximate and so cross-subsidies still appear.
elements.sent-2411-138: It is intuitively clear that this must occur for the fund to succeed in its goal of risk-sharing.
elements.sent-2411-139: The design of the fund is such that younger generations experience greater volatility in the value of their future benefits, while older generations experience a smoothing in this value.
elements.sent-2411-140: This allows the fund to invest in riskier assets while still providing a smooth income in retirement.
elements.sent-2411-141: Other CDC plans have the same feature \cite{bonenkamp,cui,gollier and they can also out-perform alternative pension options by taking on more investment risk.}
elements.sent-2411-142: In shared-indexation CDC schemes, transfer of risk between generations must imply some transfer in the market value of assets, and so some transfer of wealth must occur to achieve smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-143: The existence of these cross-subsidies is not immediately apparent from the central-estimate pricing formulae that one is required to use in valuing UK CDC funds.
elements.sent-2411-144: Instead, one must use a rigorous pricing methodology such as risk-neutral pricing to compute them.
elements.sent-2411-145: This source of cross-subsidies will occur in both single- and multi-employer schemes, but is more apparent in multi-employer schemes where it is the main source of intergenerational cross-subsidy.
elements.sent-2411-146: These cross-subsidies are stochastic and tend to cancel to a large extent over a lifetimes' investment.
elements.sent-2411-147: Nevertheless, we find that in any given year these subsidies may be surprisingly large, with some generations losing up to 40\% of the value of their contributions in some years.
elements.sent-2411-148: This implies that a significant employer subsidy may be required to ensure that it is always in each generations interest to invest in a multi-employer shared-indexation scheme.
elements.sent-2411-149: An additional source of intergenerational cross-subsidy in single-employer CDC schemes arises from what we will call {\em infinite-horizon effects .}
elements.sent-2411-150: A single-employer CDC scheme is designed so that if one uses a deterministic economic model, it will move into a steady-state which achieves a desired pension outcome.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, there is no reason to assume that in a steady-state the value of the pension received by each generation will match the value invested.
elements.sent-2411-152: In a steady state, it is possible for each generation to either consistently subsidize, or consistently be subsidized by subsequent generations.
elements.sent-2411-153: As a simple example, consider a situation where each generation consumes the next generations' pension investments, with the first generation also consuming their own pension investments.
elements.sent-2411-154: This would result in a steady-state over an infinite-time horizon, but if the scheme is terminated at any time, the final generation will lose out.
elements.sent-2411-155: We see that because of these infinite-horizon effects, it is possible for a CDC scheme to yield lower pension outcomes in a steady-state than an annuity, even though the CDC scheme is investing in assets with higher returns.
elements.sent-2411-156: We also evaluate the cross-subsidies that occur when actual investment returns differ from their predictions.
elements.sent-2411-157: Since the level of pensions paid out depend on future predictions of returns, poor predictions of those returns lead to pension payments which are, in hindsight, either too low or too high.
elements.sent-2411-158: Consequently, future generations have higher or lower pensions.
elements.sent-2411-159: This type of cross-subsidy can go either way: it may benefit earlier or later generations to join.
elements.sent-2411-160: However, it is also true that once the plan begins its life, the cost due to previous predictions deviating from their observed expected values could be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-161: This paper contributes to the literature in several ways.
elements.sent-2411-162: First, in Section \ref{sec:operationCDC , we describe an explicit mathematical model for shared-indexation CDC schemes.}
elements.sent-2411-163: This model describes the contract of such a scheme.
elements.sent-2411-164: Once the realised contributions and investments are known, this model describes what the resulting benefits will be.
elements.sent-2411-165: In the case of the multi-employer CDC scheme, this therefore gives a precise operationalisation of the broad shared understanding among pension professionals of what a multi-employer CDC scheme should entail.
elements.sent-2411-166: In Section \ref{sec:regulations , we explain briefly how our understanding of CDC funds corresponds to the UK regulations.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Second, in Section \ref{sec:esgResults , we simulate the behaviour of these funds using the stochastic economic model described in \cite{armstrongMaffraPennanen .}}
elements.sent-2411-168: This allows us to compare the outcomes of these schemes with other possible investment choices such as DC funds and a pooled annuity fund.
elements.sent-2411-169: Third, in Section \ref{sec:crossSubsidy , we study the extent of intergenerational cross subsidy in CDC funds.}
elements.sent-2411-170: To do this we use the much simpler Black--Scholes model to generate stock-price scenarios and assume other risk-factors grow deterministically.
elements.sent-2411-171: The advantage of this model is that one can compute unambiguous market values for any derivative product, and this includes the income stream one derives from a CDC pension.
elements.sent-2411-172: This allows us to quantify which generations do best from a CDC scheme and which do worst.
elements.sent-2411-173: We also derive closed-form formulae for the intergenerational cross-subsidies and infinite-horizon effects that occur in DB schemes.
elements.sent-2411-174: These provide a useful benchmark for the performance of the single-employer scheme and a simple model that explains why the cross-subsidies and infinite-horizon effects are larger in single-employer CDC schemes.
elements.sent-2411-175: Our key findings are that: multi-employer shared-indexation schemes are not able to target a given level of annual pension increases as effectively as single-employer schemes; multi-employer shared-indexation schemes have substantially lower intergenerational cross-subsidies than single-employer schemes; multi-employer schemes still feature some level of intergenerational cross-subsidy as a result of the use of approximate pricing formulae in the design of CDC funds; single-employer schemes do not necessarily outpeform annuities; multi-employer schemes can markedly outperform single-employer schemes because they do not experience the same infinite-horizon effects.
elements.section-2411-007: The operation of shared-indexation CDC funds
elements.sent-2411-176: In this section, we describe in precise mathematical terms the operation of what we call shared-indexation CDC funds.
elements.sent-2411-177: Some aspects of the operation are common to both single- and multi-employer funds and we describe these first.
elements.sent-2411-178: We then explain the different approaches taken in the single- and multi-employer cases.

elements.sent-2411-478: The single-employer CDC fund seeks to mirror the high levels of intergenerational cross subsidy seen in DB schemes, but we find that the intergenerational cross subsidy is greater in the single-employer CDC scheme.
elements.sent-2411-479: The single-employer scheme smooths income in retirement.
elements.sent-2411-480: It can lead to a higher average income in retirement than DC + Annuity, but this will not necessarily occur due to infinite-horizon effects.
elements.sent-2411-481: While such effects also exist in DB schemes, they will be small.
elements.sent-2411-482: In order to reproduce similar levels of cross subsidy to the DB schemes they replace, single-employer CDC schemes would need to offer varying benefit entitlements by age.
elements.sent-2411-483: This should reduce the infinite-horizon issues experienced in these funds and result in higher average retirement incomes.
elements.sent-2411-484: The multi-employer fund does not target a specific benefit level in the same way as a single-employer scheme, and this makes it harder to control factors such as the frequency of benefit cuts or the median annual rate of pension increase.
elements.sent-2411-485: The multi-employer CDC design results in much lower intergenerational cross subsidies than a single-employer scheme, though they are still present.
elements.sent-2411-486: The level of cross subsidies in multi-employer schemes are difficult to predict as they arise from differences between the risk-neutral and best-estimate pricing methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-487: As a result, one cannot apply the intuitions of best-estimate pricing to determine what these cross-subsidies are likely to be.
elements.sent-2411-488: Since the multi-employer scheme has much lower intergenerational cross subsidies than a single-employer scheme, the infinite-horizon effects are small.
elements.sent-2411-489: This explains why the average retirement income is higher in multi-employer schemes.
elements.sent-2411-490: This paper only considers shared-indexation CDC schemes.
elements.sent-2411-491: Some industry figures we spoke to suggested that giving all generations the same indexation was a form of ``fairness'', but we believe that, due to duration effects, shared-indexation represents a transfer of risk between generations and so inherently also represents a transfer of wealth between generations.
elements.sent-2411-492: Instead, the primary sense in which CDC schemes are fair, is simply a consequence of the fact that the same rules are applied over time.
elements.sent-2411-493: This means, that once a steady-state is achieved, different generations will receive outcomes with a similar probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-494: This is a very weak form of fairness, and even this is only guaranteed at the beginning of the scheme, and not at the time an employee joins the scheme.
elements.sent-2411-495: Multi-employer schemes do additionally ensure (at least approximately), that in the steady-state each generation receives, on average, the total amount they put in.
elements.sent-2411-496: Single-employer schemes do not provide this approximate guarantee.
elements.sent-2411-497: By contrast, the pooled annuity fund gives an example of a collective scheme which provides a much stronger fairness guarantee: for all generations, the value of the cashflows received is equal to the value of the cashflows invested.

elements.sent-2411-534: We give a very brief account of the risk-neutral pricing methodology for derivatives traded in the Black--Scholes model.
elements.sent-2411-535: The theory of pricing in these models was developed in \cite{blackScholes using partial differential equation methods.}
elements.sent-2411-536: Their approach was subsequently given a probabilistic interpretation, with the papers \cite{harrisonKreps and \cite{harrisonPliska providing a thorough account of the resulting theory of risk-neutral pricing in markets driven by diffusion processes.}}
elements.sent-2411-537: The theory is mathematically sophisticated, but is a standard topic in financial mathematics postgraduate courses and is described in many textbooks such as \cite{turner2023stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-538: In this section we will describe {\em how to price derivatives in the Black--Scholes model, but we will not attempt to justify why the methodology is correct, beyond the briefest remarks.}
elements.sent-2411-539: In the Black--Scholes model, one assumes that the logarithm of the stock price, $z_t$, can be simulated using the following recursive formula: \begin{equation}z_t = z_{t-\delta t} + \left(\mu - \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right) \delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\delta t}\, \epsilon_t \label{eq:pMeasure}\end{equation} where $\delta t$ is a chosen time step, $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are constants and the $\epsilon_t$ are independent, identically distributed standard normal random variables.
elements.sent-2411-540: The constant $\sigma$ represents the volatility of the stock and $\mu$ determines the expected returns.
elements.sent-2411-541: The $\frac{1 {2 \sigma^2$ is chosen to ensure that the expected stock price at time $t$ is $\exp(z_0 + \mu t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-542: This model is called the {\em physical probability model and it represents our assumptions about the probabilities of different scenarios occurring.}
elements.sent-2411-543: The only other asset in the Black--Scholes model is a risk-free bond which grows at a continuously compounded rate of $r$.
elements.sent-2411-544: It is assumed in the Black--Scholes model that you can trade in continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-545: In other words, one can trade on any discrete-time grid and one can also consider limiting strategies as $\delta t \to 0$.
elements.sent-2411-546: A derivative contract with maturity $T$ on the stock is simply some chosen function of the path ${\bf z :=(z_t)_{t \in [0,T]}$.}
elements.sent-2411-547: In practice, a derivative can only depend upon the values of $z$ at discrete time points.
elements.sent-2411-548: We will assume these points all lie on an evenly spaced grid $\{0, \delta t, 2 \delta t, \ldots, T\}$.
elements.sent-2411-549: The theory of pricing in the Black--Scholes model shows that, subject to extremely mild technical conditions, the price of any derivative can be computed by simulating the payoff using a slightly different probability model called the {\em pricing probability model or the {\em risk-neutral model.}}
elements.sent-2411-550: In the pricing probability model one simulates the stock price using the following recursion formula: \begin{equation}z_t = z_{t-\delta t} + \left(r - \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2\right) \delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\delta t}\, \epsilon_t \label{eq:qMeasure}\end{equation} Note that the difference between equation \eqref{eq:pMeasure and equation \eqref{eq:qMeasure is that we have replaced $\mu$ with the risk-free rate $r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-551: According to the theory of risk-neutral pricing, the price of a derivative with payoff function $f(z_0, z_{\delta t , z_{2 \delta t , \ldots, z_T)$ is equal to: \begin{equation}E^{\mathbb Q}( e^{-rT} f(z_0, z_{\delta t}, z_{2 \delta t}, \ldots, z_T) ) \label{eq:riskNeutral}\end{equation} where $E^{\mathbb Q}$ denotes the expectation with respect to the risk-neutral model.}}
elements.formula-2411-065: \begin{equation}E^{\mathbb Q}( e^{-rT} f(z_0, z_{\delta t}, z_{2 \delta t}, \ldots, z_T) ) \label{eq:riskNeutral}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-552: This should be contrasted with $E^{\mathbb P}$ which represents the actual expected value of the payoff, which will normally be different.
elements.sent-2411-553: This makes it very easy to price derivatives by Monte Carlo methods.
elements.sent-2411-554: Simply simulate a large number of stock price paths in the risk-neutral measure and use this to approximate the expected value of $f$ in the risk-neutral measure.
elements.sent-2411-555: This is how we have computed the value of the payoff from CDC contracts in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-556: We have used the Central Limit Theorem to estimate confidence intervals for the values we compute.
elements.sent-2411-557: These Monte-Carlo pricing calculations are simple to code, but naturally one asks why the pricing formula \eqref{eq:riskNeutral is correct.}
elements.sent-2411-559: The essential idea is to show that it is possible to replicate any derivative payoff by trading only in the stock and the risk-neutral bond, so long as one is allowed to trade in continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-560: This is the approach Black and Scholes took, and this allowed them to show that the price of derivatives can be computed using partial differential equation methods.
elements.sent-2411-561: A celebrated theorem due to Feynman and Kac shows that one can compute certain partial differential equations by Monte Carlo methods.
elements.sent-2411-562: If one applies their theorem to Black and Scholes' partial differential equation one obtains equation \eqref{eq:riskNeutral .}
elements.sent-2411-563: What is important is that as one can replicate any derivative at a cost given by the risk-neutral price, this means that it is the only possible price for the derivative in any market that is free from arbitrage.

elements.sent-2411-581: However, nominal benefit amounts before retirement are not equal to projected benefits and should not be presented to members as though they are equal.
elements.sent-2411-582: If changes are presented in terms of projected benefits, this apparent smoothing will largely disappear.
elements.sent-2411-583: For both types of scheme it is important to distinguish between nominal benefits and projected benefits.
elements.sent-2411-584: Nominal benefits should not be presented to members as though they were projected benefits.
elements.sent-2411-585: We evaluate the performance and level of intergenerational cross-subsidy in flat-accrual and dynamic-accrual collective defined contribution (CDC) schemes which have been designed to be compatible with UK legislation.
elements.sent-2411-586: In the flat-accrual scheme, all members accrue the benefits at the same rate irrespective of age.
elements.sent-2411-587: This captures the most significant feature of the Royal Mail Collective Pension Plan, which is currently the only UK CDC scheme.
elements.sent-2411-588: The dynamic-accrual schemes seeks to reduce intergenerational cross-subsidies by varying the rate of benefit-accrual in accordance to the age of members and the current funding level.
elements.sent-2411-589: We find that these CDC schemes can often be successful in smoothing pension outcomes post-retirement while outperforming a defined contribution scheme followed by annuity purchase at the point of retirement.
elements.sent-2411-590: However, this out-performance is not guaranteed in a flat-accrual scheme and there is little smoothing of projected pension outcomes before retirement.
elements.sent-2411-591: There are significant intergenerational cross-subsidies in the flat-accrual scheme.
elements.sent-2411-592: These qualitatively mirror the cross-subsidies seen in existing defined benefit schemes, but we find the magnitude of the cross-subsidies is much larger in flat-accrual CDC schemes.
elements.sent-2411-593: The dynamic-accrual scheme design is intended to reduce such cross-subsidies, but we find they still arise due to the approximate pricing methodology used to determine the benefits accrued by each contribution.
elements.sent-2411-594: Although the cross-subsidies tend to cancel out over time, in any given year they can be large.
elements.sent-2411-595: Thus, the benefits accrued by contributions should be calculated rigorously to reduce cross-subsidies.
elements.sent-2411-596: Collective defined contribution (CDC) pension schemes have arrived in the UK, after a long and fragile genesis \cite{wilkinson .}
elements.sent-2411-597: These are pension schemes which share risks, such as longevity, investment and inflation risk, collectively among the scheme members.
elements.sent-2411-598: This is different to individual defined contribution (DC) pension schemes, where members bear only their own risk.
elements.sent-2411-599: Unlike defined benefit (DB) pension schemes, the other main form of pension provision in the UK, benefit payments in CDC schemes are not guaranteed; indeed, they are expected to fluctuate.
elements.sent-2411-600: It is important to understand what risks are being borne by which members in CDC schemes and to quantify them, to reduce and mitigate against unfairness between different members.
elements.sent-2411-601: We study two different whole-life CDC scheme designs which would comply with the current UK regulations.
elements.sent-2411-602: The collective risk-sharing is done through declaring the same increases on all members' benefits, a UK regulatory requirement for CDC schemes.
elements.sent-2411-603: We refer to such schemes as {\em shared-indexation schemes to highlight this common feature of their design.}
elements.sent-2411-604: The first shared-indexation scheme is a flat-accrual scheme, in which the contributions paid into the scheme are a fixed percentage of salaries.
elements.sent-2411-605: In exchange, they receive a deferred retirement income linked to their current salary.
elements.sent-2411-606: This scheme is closely modelled on that of the Royal Mail Collective Pension Plan (RMCPP), the first, and currently only, UK CDC scheme in existence.
elements.sent-2411-607: The design of RMCPP has strong similarities with the defined benefit scheme it replaces.
elements.sent-2411-608: The desire to launch the RMCPP has been the driving force behind the introduction of the legislation enabling CDC schemes in the UK \cite{wilkinson .}
elements.sent-2411-609: Combined with the scale of the scheme, which is anticipated to have over 100,000 members, the importance and significance of the RMCPP is clear.
elements.sent-2411-610: It is the prototype for CDC plan design in the UK, for UK regulations and regulators, for actuaries, employers and workers' unions.
elements.sent-2411-611: The second shared-indexation scheme we consider is a dynamic-accrual CDC scheme.
elements.sent-2411-612: In such a scheme, members can choose how much to contribute and, in return, receive a deferred retirement income that reflects financial market conditions at the time the contribution was paid.
elements.sent-2411-613: While no such scheme exists in the UK at present, in 2024 the UK Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) held a consultation on multi-employer CDC and our dynamic-accrual scheme is intended to reflect a common understanding of UK pension professionals on how such a scheme might operate.
elements.sent-2411-614: To achieve this, we have worked with the Pensions Policy Institute, a not-for-profit UK research institute, who have conducted a series of interviews on CDC pensions with a broad range of stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-615: They have used these insights to publish two briefing notes on CDC schemes \cite{wilkinson,upton .}
elements.sent-2411-616: The aim of this paper is to compare these two scheme designs and evaluate the extent to which they succeed in design goals such as providing an income for life, minimizing intergenerational unfairness and smoothing pension outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-617: Due to the novelty of UK CDC schemes, with the RMCPP launched in October 2024, there is little literature on their unique design.
elements.sent-2411-618: A 2023 report prepared for the ABI \cite{abi summarises numerous industry studies of similar designs \cite{governmentActuaries, aon, popat, abi, irri, wtw , as well as providing its own additional analysis.}}
elements.sent-2411-619: However, there are important differences in details between the scheme designs in these studies and the RMCPP.
elements.sent-2411-620: Notable differences include the use of buffers and funding corridors.
elements.sent-2411-621: Of these studies, only two perform stochastic modelling \cite{governmentActuaries,popat .}
elements.sent-2411-622: In the academic literature, Owadally et al. \cite{owadally discuss CDC plans in the UK with respect to policy, as well as their risks and the ensuing advantages and disadvantages, and model an earlier proposal stochastically.}
elements.sent-2411-623: They also compare the outcomes for a single member of their CDC plan model against typical DC-based alternative pension schemes, when the scheme is mature.
elements.sent-2411-624: In the case of the dynamic-accrual CDC scheme, this gives a precise operationalisation of the broad shared understanding among pension professionals of what a dynamic-accrual CDC scheme should entail.
elements.sent-2411-625: In describing a flat-accrual scheme, we follow the key design features of the RMCPP, but there are differences which we now summarise.
elements.sent-2411-626: Only a single-life pension in retirement is valued in our model -- spousal benefits, death benefits and the UK's tax-free cash lump sum payment made at the point of retirement are omitted.
elements.sent-2411-627: We include an upper bound on indexation which is not present in the RMCPP, but is required for dynamic-accrual and so aids comparison between the two scheme types.
elements.sent-2411-628: We assume that benefit cuts and bonuses that must occur when the fund is significantly over- or under-funded take effect immediately, rather than over time as is done for benefit cuts in the RMCPP.
elements.sent-2411-629: In our model, the relationship between contribution rate and level of benefits is determined by choosing a long-term target rate of indexation.
elements.sent-2411-630: In the RMCPP, these levels are fixed in the Trust Deed and Rules \cite{royalMailTrustDeeds , but it is not specified how they are determined.}
elements.sent-2411-631: Nevertheless, the member handbook does suggest that ``The aim is to provide increases to help keep up with the cost of living'' \cite[p.\ 67]{royalMailHandbook .}
elements.sent-2411-632: We endeavour to make a clear separation between the contract of a CDC fund and our modelling assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-634: Section \ref{sec:modelling describes modelling assumptions including demographic and economic factors.}
elements.sent-2411-635: Having established the contract of CDC funds, the second way our paper contributes to the literature is by studying the extent of intergenerational cross-subsidy in CDC funds.
elements.sent-2411-636: This is the topic of Section \ref{sec:crossSubsidy .}
elements.sent-2411-637: We find that flat-accrual schemes and dynamic-accrual schemes contain very different levels of cross-subsidies.
elements.sent-2411-638: A dominant source of cross-subsidy in the flat-accrual scheme results from the combination of a fixed contribution rate and fixed accrual rate.
elements.sent-2411-639: This favours participants who are close to retirement as they receive the same benefit amount as younger members for the same contribution, despite there being less time for their investments to grow.
elements.sent-2411-640: This qualitatively mirrors the behaviour of the DB scheme that the RMCPP flat-accrual scheme is designed to replace.
elements.sent-2411-641: However, when examined quantitatively, we find that the level of cross-subsidy is considerably higher in a flat-accrual scheme than a DB scheme.
elements.sent-2411-642: Since the earliest generations in a flat-accrual scheme receive an income that is worth more than the value of their lifetime contributions, subsequent generations must receive an income that is worth less than their contributions.
elements.sent-2411-643: We find that the flat-accrual scheme broadly tends to a long-term steady-state situation where typical generations receive a retirement income worth less than their contributions.
elements.sent-2411-644: One can think of this as later generations paying interest on the debt built up by overpaying the earliest generations.
elements.sent-2411-645: We refer to this effect as ``drag''.
elements.sent-2411-646: It may appear counter-intuitive that a scheme can enter a steady-state which is not actuarially fair.
elements.sent-2411-647: This is possible because over an infinite time horizon one cannot argue that the total money paid into the scheme must equal the total money paid out, so we will also refer to this as an ``infinite-horizon effect''.
elements.sent-2411-648: The dynamic-accrual scheme is designed to avoid the obvious cross-subsidies of a flat-accrual scheme.
elements.sent-2411-649: It is instead intended to be actuarially fair, in the sense that members' contributions match their new benefit entitlements, at the time of each contribution.
elements.sent-2411-650: However, the implicit pricing methodology that has been proposed in industry to do this is only approximate, and so cross-subsidies still appear.
elements.sent-2411-651: These cross-subsidies are stochastic in nature, meaning one cannot predict in advance which generations will lose out.
elements.sent-2411-652: We briefly consider two ways to mitigate the cross-subsidies we have identified.
elements.sent-2411-653: If a flat-accrual scheme is designed to replace a DB scheme, one might wish to design a scheme that has similar levels of cross-subsidy to the DB scheme it seeks to replace.
elements.sent-2411-654: This could be done using age-based-accrual rather than flat-accrual.
elements.sent-2411-655: In a dynamic-accrual scheme, we show how to statistically calibrate a pricing formula to obtain fairer prices for benefits than the formula proposed by industry.
elements.sent-2411-656: These mitigations are able to manage the level of cross-subsidies without departing from a shared-indexation design, but do not eliminate them entirely.
elements.sent-2411-657: We use two distinct methodologies to examine the cross-subsidies.
elements.sent-2411-658: The cross-subsidies in a flat-accrual scheme and the notion of drag can be studied using a constant economic model.
elements.sent-2411-659: This allows us to find analytic results.
elements.sent-2411-660: However, the cross-subsidies in a dynamic-accrual scheme require stochastic modelling.
elements.sent-2411-661: In this case, we use a Black-Scholes economic model in order to ensure that any pension income can be priced unambiguously using the theory of risk-neutral pricing.
elements.sent-2411-662: The cross-subsidies then emerge from the difference between the true risk-neutral price and the ``small-noise'' approximation to the price used to determine how much members are charged for their pension benefits.
elements.sent-2411-663: In the case of flat-accrual schemes, stochastic modelling confirms the results obtained using a constant economic model.
elements.sent-2411-664: Cross-subsidies have been studied in other types of CDC scheme, and the high cross-subsidies and consequent drag are a specific consequence of the flat-accrual design.
elements.sent-2411-665: For example, \cite{haan studies a Dutch-style CDC scheme and shows a greater financial fairness of those pension schemes.}
elements.sent-2411-666: The third way we contribute to the literature, is by simulating the behaviour of these funds using a stochastic economic model and comparing the performance to other scheme designs.
elements.sent-2411-667: This is done in Section \ref{sec:esgResults , using the model described in \cite{armstrongMaffraPennanen .}}
elements.sent-2411-668: We compare the performance with the alternatives of DC schemes and a pooled annuity fund.
elements.sent-2411-669: Similar stochastic models have been used to study CDC designs that have been proposed for use in the UK e.g.\ \cite{popat, owadally , but not for the current shared-indexation design of the RMCPP.}
elements.sent-2411-670: In all our modelling in this paper, we assume there is no systematic longevity risk and there are sufficient numbers of members so that individual longevity risk is eliminated through pooling.
elements.sent-2411-671: We do not allow for any heterogeneity in the schemes in our simulations.
elements.sent-2411-672: However, we do show how heterogeneity can be incorporated into the scheme design if desired; results from simulating heterogeneous funds are discussed in \cite{upton .}
elements.sent-2411-673: The fourth contribution to the literature is studying the extent to which shared-indexation schemes are able to smooth pension incomes.
elements.sent-2411-675: The design of shared-indexation schemes is such that younger generations experience greater volatility in the value of their future benefits, while older generations experience a smoothing in this value.
elements.sent-2411-676: Other CDC plans studied in the literature have the same feature and they can also out-perform alternative pension options by taking on more investment risk \cite{bonenkamp,cui,gollier .}
elements.sent-2411-677: We find that the shared-indexation designs do provide some smoothing of benefits after retirement, but they do not smooth projected benefits before retirement by as much as has been suggested \cite{aon .}
elements.sent-2411-678: The discrepancy between our results and the literature can be attributed to the fact that none of the studies we examined attempted to compute projected benefits using a stochastic model, and the deterministic approximations used to project benefits are not sufficiently accurate for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-679: Our fifth contribution is in Section \ref{sec:cap2 , where we consider how a scheme designer might control the outcomes in a CDC fund.}
elements.sent-2411-680: We find that it is quite easy in a flat-accrual scheme to target a particular growth rate for median incomes in retirement by adjusting the relationship between benefits and contributions.
elements.sent-2411-681: This flexibility is not available in a dynamic-accrual scheme, making it difficult to design a scheme so that it will produce a desired level of indexation.
elements.sent-2411-682: Our final contribution, in Section \ref{sec:modelRisk , is to briefly consider model risk by evaluating the cross-subsidies that occur when actual investment returns differ from their predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-683: Since the level of pensions paid out depend on future predictions of returns, poor predictions of those returns lead to pension payments that are, in hindsight, either too low or too high.
elements.section-2411-016: The contract of shared-indexation CDC funds
elements.sent-2411-685: In this section, we detail the contractual aspects of shared-indexation CDC funds.
elements.sent-2411-686: The aspects of their operation which are common to both flat- and dynamic-accrual funds, namely the annual indexation of accrued pensions are described first.
elements.sent-2411-687: We then explain the different approaches taken in the flat- and dynamic-accrual CDC funds to the accrual of benefits.
elements.sent-2411-688: In Appendix \ref{sec:regulations , we explain briefly how our description of CDC funds corresponds to the UK regulations.}

elements.section-2411-022: Scheme behaviour when investment returns are not as expected
elements.sent-2411-1152: In this case, one sees that if stock returns are greater than expected, later generations in the flat-accrual scheme benefit more than earlier generations.
elements.sent-2411-1153: In the dynamic-accrual scheme, the fund responds in a way that appears broadly consistent with actuarial fairness: the longer an individual invests their funds, the more they benefit from unexpectedly high stock prices.
elements.sent-2411-1154: The flat-accrual CDC fund seeks to mirror the high levels of intergenerational cross-subsidy seen in DB schemes, but we find this subsidy is approximately four times greater in the flat-accrual CDC scheme.
elements.sent-2411-1155: The flat-accrual scheme smooths income in retirement.
elements.sent-2411-1156: It can lead to a higher average income in retirement than DC+annuity, but this will not necessarily occur due to infinite-horizon effects.
elements.sent-2411-1157: In order to reproduce similar levels of cross-subsidy to the DB schemes they replace, a better alternative is to offer varying benefit entitlements by age.
elements.sent-2411-1158: This reduces the infinite-horizon issues experienced in these funds and result in higher average retirement incomes.
elements.sent-2411-1159: The standard dynamic-accrual fund does not target a specific benefit level in the same way as a flat-accrual scheme, and this makes it harder to control factors such as the frequency of benefit cuts or the median annual rate of pension increase.
elements.sent-2411-1160: The dynamic-accrual CDC design results in much lower intergenerational cross-subsidies than a flat-accrual scheme, though they are still present.
elements.sent-2411-1161: Much of the level of cross-subsidies in dynamic-accrual schemes can be predicted using a linear model in $h$ and age, with a path-dependent residual.
elements.sent-2411-1162: Using this linear model to match benefits and contributions reduces the pricing errors.
elements.sent-2411-1163: Because the dynamic-accrual scheme has much lower intergenerational cross-subsidies than a flat-accrual scheme, the infinite-horizon effects are small.
elements.sent-2411-1164: This explains why the average retirement income is higher in dynamic-accrual schemes.
